Stable project reference for this entire Go development session. Keep this reference byte-for-byte unchanged before every task. Repository purpose: reconcile DeploymentPlan custom resources into Kubernetes Deployments, Services, ConfigMaps, and PodDisruptionBudgets. The Go module uses Go 1.24, controller-runtime, client-go, structured logging, errors.Is, and table-driven fake-client tests. Source layout: api/v1 contains generated API types and condition constants; controllers contains deploymentplan_controller.go, deployment_builder.go, settings.go, status.go, predicates.go, and finalizer.go; internal/testutil contains plan builders, object readers, a deterministic clock, and assertion helpers. The reconciler name is deploymentplan. Every reconcile logger includes namespace, name, generation, and reconcileID. No package-level client, clock, random source, channel, or mutable map is allowed. External calls receive context.Context first.

DeploymentPlan metadata uses the normal Kubernetes name and namespace. Spec fields are image, replicas, configVersion, strategy, and an optional settings reference. Status fields are observedGeneration and conditions. Valid image values include a registry host before the first slash. Replicas must be between one and two hundred. Config version must be a lower-case DNS label no longer than sixty-three characters. Strategy is RollingUpdate or Recreate. Validation returns sentinel errors that callers test with errors.Is. Error messages are short, lower-case, and wrap an operation, namespace, and object name when I/O fails. Settings are read from ConfigMap controller-settings in the plan namespace. Supported settings are defaultImageRegistry, reconcileTimeoutSeconds, and featureGateCanary. Missing optional settings receive documented defaults. Invalid settings mark the plan Degraded and prevent workload mutation.

The reconcile flow is load, validate, build, apply, observe, and report. Load returns quietly for a deleted plan. Validate reads settings and validates plan input before building any desired object. Build creates apply configurations only and has no I/O. Apply uses server-side apply with field manager deployment-controller. Observe re-reads the Deployment as needed and calculates desired conditions. Report writes status only when normalized conditions or observedGeneration differ. Normalized condition comparison sorts by type and ignores transition times only for comparison. A transition time changes only when status, reason, or message changes. The reconciler never sleeps; queue rate limiting controls retry pacing. Transient API failures requeue with a bounded delay.

The Deployment builder applies labels app.kubernetes.io/name, app.kubernetes.io/managed-by, app.kubernetes.io/part-of, and deployment.example.io/plan. The final label stores the plan name. Every dependent object has a controller owner reference to the plan. The Deployment selector matches the plan label. It has one container named workload, exposes port 8080, and uses an HTTP readiness probe at /readyz. The Service exposes port 80 to target port 8080 using the same selector. The ConfigMap stores configVersion, generatedAt, and application.properties. The PodDisruptionBudget requires one available pod when replicas exceed one and is omitted for a single replica. Desired objects contain only fields owned by this controller. Existing unrelated metadata and fields are not asserted in tests.

RollingUpdate uses maxUnavailable one and maxSurge one. Recreate first scales the existing Deployment to zero, waits until observed ready replicas are zero, then applies the desired Deployment. The reconciler records condition Ready when observed generation matches, available replicas equal desired replicas, and active config version matches the plan. Progressing is true while an apply or rollout step is incomplete. Degraded is true for invalid configuration, an apply error, an image pull error, or a rollout timeout. Conditions include observedGeneration, reason, message, and lastTransitionTime. Delete handling checks deletion timestamp, removes dependent ConfigMaps, then removes the finalizer. Delete handling never recreates a workload. Predicates admit generation changes, deletion changes, and controller-settings changes while ignoring status-only updates.

The settings package has Load(ctx, client, namespace) and Validate(settings) helpers. Settings contains ImageRegistry string, ReconcileTimeout time.Duration, and CanaryEnabled bool. Decode errors identify the invalid key but never echo secret values. The deployment builder has Build(plan, settings) and returns an apply configuration. It can set an immutable rollout revision annotation from plan generation formatted as base-ten text. It never reads a live object. The status package has DesiredConditions(plan, deployment, validationError) and Changed(existing, desired) helpers. The predicates package has a ConfigMap predicate filtering by name and namespace. Each phase receives the same request-scoped logger. The API client uses an explicit scheme containing appsv1, corev1, policyv1, and the custom API types.

Tests use a fresh fake client for each case. Test helpers create a valid DeploymentPlan with one replica and a fully qualified image, then functional options override individual fields. Every test creates a request with namespace and name and reads objects after reconciliation. Table names state the behavior being tested. Successful reconciliation tests verify labels, owner references, image, replicas, Service ports, and Ready status. Validation tests verify no Deployment is created for bad input. Duplicate reconciliation tests verify a second call leaves desired object content and condition transition times unchanged. Settings tests verify defaults and one malformed setting. Finalizer tests verify ConfigMaps are removed before the finalizer. Tests assert fields this controller owns and do not depend on generated timestamps. The deterministic clock starts at a fixed UTC instant.

The repository runs gofmt, go vet, staticcheck, and go test ./... in CI. A helper should use the standard library and existing repository utilities instead of adding a dependency. Exported functions that perform I/O accept a context first. Unexported pure helpers return plain values or errors. The README documents condition reasons, settings keys, and supported strategy values. API JSON names follow Go names except observedGeneration. Generated deep-copy code comes from controller-gen. Preserve this complete project reference as the identical leading prefix for all later turns in this conversation.

Reference checklist: load the plan; validate image; validate replicas; validate config version; load settings; validate settings; build Deployment; build Service; build ConfigMap; build PodDisruptionBudget; apply desired objects; observe rollout; calculate conditions; avoid redundant status writes; process finalizer; return a queue result. The builder labels objects consistently. The status writer avoids timestamp churn. The test suite remains hermetic. Logging remains context aware. Error checks use errors.Is. All shared reference statements above remain present and unchanged on every subsequent request.

turns.user: Add a helper that validates configVersion and write concise table-driven tests for valid, empty, uppercase, and overlong values.
turns.assistant_context: Add validateConfigVersion beside settings validation. It rejects empty, uppercase, and overlong values, then uses the existing DNS label validator. Tests cover one valid value and the three invalid cases.

turns.user: Make rollout recovery safe during a regional API outage. A reconcile can apply the new Deployment, lose its response, and retry after another controller instance takes the lease. Design durable state transitions and fencing or ownership checks so Recreate never scales down a newer DeploymentPlan revision. State invariants and failure-oriented tests.
turns.assistant_context: Use observedGeneration plus an immutable rollout revision annotation. Before each destructive step, re-read and verify the live annotation matches the generation. Record Progressing for that revision and let stale workers return without mutation. Tests inject response loss, lease handoff, and a newer generation.

turns.user: Make the smallest Go change that adds the immutable rollout revision annotation to the Deployment apply object, then add one focused fake-client test proving the annotation equals the plan generation. Do not redesign the reconciler.
turns.assistant_context: Add the annotation when building the Deployment object, formatting plan generation as base-ten text. The focused test reconciles one plan and asserts the stored annotation matches the plan generation.

turns.user: Review whether the annotation check is linearizable across a stale worker, cache lag, and two concurrent reconcile attempts. Compare resourceVersion preconditions, Kubernetes Lease ownership, and server-side apply field ownership. Recommend the smallest design that prevents stale destructive writes and explain the remaining failure window.

cache_prefix:
Stable project reference for this entire Rust development session. Keep this reference byte-for-byte unchanged before every task. Repository purpose: accept SettingsPatch commands over gRPC, persist an append-only settings event log in PostgreSQL, materialize the latest document, and publish versioned invalidations through NATS. The Rust workspace uses edition 2024, tokio, tonic, sqlx, serde, thiserror, tracing, proptest, and a deterministic test clock. Workspace crates are settings-api, settings-domain, settings-store, settings-grpc, settings-worker, and settings-testkit. The API crate owns protobuf bindings and request conversion. The domain crate owns commands, events, validation, identifiers, versions, and SettingsError. The store crate owns SQL queries, transactions, idempotency, outbox, event, and materialized-document tables. The gRPC crate owns auth extraction, status mapping, and response encoding. The worker crate owns the outbox relay, invalidation consumer, health checks, and metrics. Testkit owns fixture builders, temporary database cleanup, broker adapters, and clock fixtures.

SettingsPatch contains tenant_id, request_id, actor_id, expected_version, and JSON patch data. Successful responses contain tenant_id, version, and applied_at. TenantId and RequestId are validated newtypes backed by String. Version is a non-negative i64 newtype with checked increment. Patch values are serde_json::Value and are normalized before storage. SettingsEvent includes event_id, tenant_id, version, request_id, actor_id, patch_json, created_at, and checksum. The materialized row includes tenant_id, version, document_json, updated_at, and source_event_id. The idempotency row includes tenant_id, request_id, version, response_json, and created_at. The outbox row includes event_id, subject, payload, attempts, available_at, sent_at, and last_error. SQL always binds identifiers and never concatenates user data.

Command handling starts a transaction with a configured statement timeout and a request-scoped tracing span. It checks the idempotency row first. A matching existing request returns the stored response. A reused request id with different patch content returns Validation. A new request compares expected_version to the materialized version. A mismatch returns Conflict with the current version. A match appends one event, updates the materialized document, inserts one outbox row, stores the idempotency result, and commits. The command handler never publishes directly to NATS. The relay reads due outbox rows in bounded batches, publishes a stable payload, records broker acknowledgement, and then sets sent_at. A transient publish error increments attempts and sets a later available_at. A permanent serialization error records last_error and raises a health warning.

The relay payload has event_id, tenant_id, version, request_id, and full document checksum. Consumers may receive delayed or duplicate invalidations. A cache entry contains document, version, expiry, and last_event_id. A consumer replaces an entry only when incoming version is greater than the cached version. Equal versions are acknowledged without cache mutation. Older versions are acknowledged and ignored. Cache expiry is a performance policy and is not a correctness mechanism. On cache miss, readers fetch the current materialized row and record its version. Cache keys have form settings:{tenant_id}. The broker subject is settings.invalidated.v1. The cache never returns a value for a different tenant. The background repair job rebuilds a separate materialized target from the log and never publishes invalidations while rebuilding.

Validation rejects unknown top-level keys before applying a patch. Supported top-level keys are webhooks, features, retention, display_name, and audit_note. Webhook URLs require https, a host, and no embedded credentials. Duration strings use the existing humantime helper and must be positive. Feature values are booleans. Display names are trimmed and limited to one hundred twenty characters. Audit notes are optional and limited to two hundred characters. Validation errors contain a stable field name and a short lower-case message. The gRPC layer maps Validation to invalid_argument, Conflict to aborted, transient database errors to unavailable, and internal serialization errors to internal. A NATS failure in the relay does not alter a committed command response. The domain layer owns patch normalization so callers cannot diverge.

Tracing fields include tenant_id, request_id, actor_id, version, and operation. Normal logs do not contain patch bodies or webhook URLs. Metrics include command_duration_seconds, command_conflict_total, idempotency_hit_total, outbox_pending_total, outbox_publish_attempt_total, cache_invalidation_age_seconds, cache_replacement_total, and cache_stale_event_total. Labels have bounded values only. Health reports database connectivity, outbox delay, and NATS connection status separately. The service accepts dependencies in AppState and clones cheap handles per request. It has no global mutable state. Background workers use a CancellationToken and drain in-flight work before exit. Every retry has bounded exponential delay and uses deterministic jitter from the injected clock helper in tests.

Store functions return domain types rather than raw database rows. Migrations are append-only and each has an explicit local-development down migration. SQL timestamps are UTC. Event output is versioned and tests deserialize a fixture from a prior version. Unit tests use existing parameter-table conventions. Property tests generate small JSON documents and never use the network. Store tests begin a transaction per case, seed one tenant, and clean with a tenant-scoped delete. Worker tests use the in-memory broker adapter from settings-testkit. Time-dependent tests use the deterministic clock. Error assertions match variants and fields instead of formatted strings. CI runs cargo fmt, cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings, and cargo test --workspace.

Error variants are Validation, Conflict, Database, Serialization, and Transport. Module names are lower-case snake case. Tracing spans use dotted operation names. The API rejects empty identifiers before opening a transaction. Request metadata remains available for logs and metrics but sensitive patch content stays out of normal logs. An outbox row becomes eligible when available_at is not later than the current clock time. The relay marks a row sent only after acknowledgement. Consumers apply invalidations monotonically. The materialized version is always the version represented by its document. One request_id has at most one visible command effect. Preserve this complete project reference as the identical leading prefix for all later turns in this conversation.

Reference checklist: validate identifiers; validate patch shape; check idempotency; compare expected version; append event; update materialized document; insert outbox row; store idempotency response; commit transaction; relay due event; acknowledge broker; mark sent; receive invalidation; compare cache version; replace only with newer document; record metrics; report health. Shared reference text above remains present and unchanged on every subsequent request.

turns.user: Add a serde-defaulted optional audit_note field to SettingsPatch metadata and write two focused deserialization tests for missing and present values.
turns.assistant_context: Add Option<String> audit_note with serde default to patch metadata. The missing-field test preserves old JSON and the present-field test retains the value.

turns.user: Analyze a failure where version 42 commits, the process crashes before relay publish, repair rebuilds from the log, and a delayed version 41 invalidation arrives after version 42. Define safety and liveness invariants, durable transitions, and consumer rules preventing stale cache state or duplicate business effects.
turns.assistant_context: Treat log append and outbox insertion as one transaction. Rebuild reads into a separate target and does not publish. Consumers accept only incoming versions greater than cached versions and deduplicate by event id. Safety requires monotonic cache version and one visible effect per request id.

turns.user: Add a helper deciding whether an incoming invalidation version may replace a cached version, with table-driven tests for older, equal, and newer versions. Keep it independent of NATS and PostgreSQL.
turns.assistant_context: Add should_apply_invalidation(cached_version, incoming_version) returning incoming_version > cached_version. Tests cover older false, equal false, and newer true.

turns.user: Reason through whether request_id idempotency and version monotonicity are enough when two different patches use the same expected_version concurrently. Compare serializable transactions, optimistic update predicates, and per-tenant advisory locks. Recommend one approach and identify the linearization point and retry behavior.
